title: Prostatitis
oneLiner: Inflammation of the prostate; acute bacterial (sepsis risk) or chronic pelvic pain syndrome; common in men 30–50.

sections.Killers First – What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Sepsis from acute bacterial prostatitis**  
  – High fever, rigors, hypotension, tender boggy prostate on DRE, rising lactate.  
- **Acute urinary retention with post-renal AKI**  
  – Inability to void, suprapubic pain, palpable bladder, raised creatinine.  
- **Prostatic abscess**  
  – Persistent fever despite antibiotics, perineal pain, fluctuant prostate on DRE (or imaging).
sections.Presentation:
- **Acute:** fever, chills, perineal/pelvic pain, dysuria, frequency, urgency, urinary retention; DRE shows tender, swollen, boggy prostate.  
- **Chronic:** persistent pelvic pain ≥3 months, lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), pain after ejaculation, variable DRE (tender but not boggy).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **UTI/cystitis** – no perineal pain, prostate normal on DRE, symptoms mainly lower urinary.  
- **Pyelonephritis** – flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, higher systemic symptoms.  
- **Benign prostatic hyperplasia** – no fever, gradual LUTS, non-tender prostate.  
- **Prostate cancer** – often asymptomatic, hard nodule on DRE, raised PSA (careful: PSA can be elevated in prostatitis).  
- **Urethritis** – urethral discharge, dysuria, STI history, prostate non-tender.  
- **Splitter:** DRE – tender, swollen boggy prostate suggests acute prostatitis; firm, nodular suggests malignancy; normal suggests non-prostatic cause.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** urine dip (nitrite, leucocytes, blood), DRE (if no contraindications), bladder scan for residual volume, urinalysis.  
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U&Es, creatinine, blood cultures (if febrile/septic), PSA (consider but note false elevation).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) if suspicion of abscess or refractory symptoms.  
  - Second-line: CT pelvis if systemic unwell or abscess suspected.  
- **Special tests:** STI screening (Chlamydia, gonorrhoea) if at risk – first-catch urine NAAT. Mid-stream urine culture and sensitivity.  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis based on symptoms, DRE, urine culture. For chronic prostatitis, NIH Chronic Prostatitis Symptom Index (CPSI) can quantify symptoms.  
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures before antibiotics in febrile/septic patient. Also, PSA should be interpreted with caution (elevated in prostatitis; recheck after treatment).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If sepsis: IV fluids, IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2g IV per local sepsis protocol), catheter if retention (prefer suprapubic to avoid urethral trauma), analgesia.  
  - Send cultures before antibiotics if possible – do NOT delay if shocked.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Switch to oral antibiotics when stable (ciprofloxacin or co-trimoxazole).  
  - Consider alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) if retention/voiding difficulty.  
  - NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) for pain.  
  - Monitor temperature, urinary symptoms, repeat urine culture if no response.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Complete antibiotic course: acute 10–14 days (severe/chronic 14–28 days); chronic prostatitis 4–6 weeks.  
  - Alpha-blocker for LUTS, referral to urology if recurrent/no improvement.  
  - Lifestyle advice: avoid caffeine/alcohol, cycling, prolonged sitting; sitz baths.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 ml/hr (adjust for shock/renal).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if >60 or reduced mobility (check bleeding risk).  
  - Diet: oral fluids encouraged unless NBM for procedure.  
  - Observations: baseline then 4–6 hourly (NEWS2 q1h if septic, then reduce).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (acute bacterial):**  
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD for 14 days.  
  - Alternative: ofloxacin 200 mg PO BD for 14 days.  
  - If fluoroquinolone contraindicated: co-trimoxazole 960 mg PO BD for 14 days (check local resistance).  
  - For sepsis (IV): ceftriaxone 2 g IV OD ± gentamicin [check local policy].  
- **Chronic prostatitis:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD or ofloxacin 200 mg PO BD for 4–6 weeks.  
  - Or doxycycline 100 mg PO BD for 4 weeks if atypical organisms suspected.  
- **Alpha-blocker:** tamsulosin 400 micrograms PO OD for LUTS/retention (usually 4–6 weeks).  
- **NSAID:** ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS with food, or naproxen 250–500 mg PO BD. Consider PPI cover if risk.  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin – avoid in epilepsy, tendon disorders, pregnancy, children; risk of tendon rupture (especially elderly + steroids).  
  - Co-trimoxazole – caution in elderly, renal impairment, folate deficiency; avoid with ACE-i/ARBs (hyperkalemia).  
  - Tamsulosin – postural hypotension, intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (cataract surgery).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g., 250 mg BD).  
  - Co-trimoxazole: avoid if CrCl <15, adjust if <30.  
  - NSAIDs: avoid/use caution in renal impairment.  
  - Tamsulosin: caution in hepatic impairment.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin – tendinopathy / QT prolongation; avoid concurrent QT-prolonging drugs.  
  - Co-trimoxazole – hyperkalemia with ACE-i/ARBs.  
  - Tamsulosin – first-dose hypotension (take at bedtime).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** prostatic abscess (drainage needed), sepsis/septic shock, acute urinary retention, AKI, epididymo-orchitis, bacteraemia.  
- **Chronic:** chronic pelvic pain syndrome, recurrent UTIs, sexual dysfunction, infertility, psychological distress, prostatic calcifications.
sections.Prognosis:
- Acute bacterial prostatitis: good with appropriate antibiotics; recurrence ~10–20%.  
- Chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome: often persistent; up to 50% have ongoing symptoms at 1 year despite treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always perform DRE in suspected prostatitis (after checking for contraindicating signs like cauda equina).  
- Send urine culture before starting antibiotics – but do NOT delay if septic.  
- Use suprapubic catheter (or smallest urethral catheter) for acute urinary retention to reduce trauma/bacteraemia.  
- Recheck PSA 6 weeks after finishing antibiotics to reset baseline.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do NOT massage the prostate vigorously during DRE in acute prostatitis – can cause bacteraemia.  
- Do NOT delay antibiotics in a septic patient to wait for cultures – give empiric IV broad-spectrum after blood cultures.  
- Do NOT use a large-bore urethral catheter in retention – prefer suprapubic or small temporary catheter.  
- Do NOT assume chronic prostatitis is always bacterial – many cases are non-bacterial chronic pelvic pain syndrome; avoid repeated long antibiotic courses.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever persists >72 hours on oral antibiotics; unable to pass urine; severe perineal pain; signs of sepsis (rigors, confusion, hypotension); new back pain or leg weakness (suspect spinal/epidural abscess).  
- **Follow-up:** Urology outpatient in 4

rawMarkdown:
## Prostatitis
**One-liner:** Inflammation of the prostate; acute bacterial (sepsis risk) or chronic pelvic pain syndrome; common in men 30–50.

### Killers First – What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Sepsis from acute bacterial prostatitis**  
  – High fever, rigors, hypotension, tender boggy prostate on DRE, rising lactate.  
- **Acute urinary retention with post-renal AKI**  
  – Inability to void, suprapubic pain, palpable bladder, raised creatinine.  
- **Prostatic abscess**  
  – Persistent fever despite antibiotics, perineal pain, fluctuant prostate on DRE (or imaging).

### Presentation
- **Acute:** fever, chills, perineal/pelvic pain, dysuria, frequency, urgency, urinary retention; DRE shows tender, swollen, boggy prostate.  
- **Chronic:** persistent pelvic pain ≥3 months, lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), pain after ejaculation, variable DRE (tender but not boggy).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **UTI/cystitis** – no perineal pain, prostate normal on DRE, symptoms mainly lower urinary.  
- **Pyelonephritis** – flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, higher systemic symptoms.  
- **Benign prostatic hyperplasia** – no fever, gradual LUTS, non-tender prostate.  
- **Prostate cancer** – often asymptomatic, hard nodule on DRE, raised PSA (careful: PSA can be elevated in prostatitis).  
- **Urethritis** – urethral discharge, dysuria, STI history, prostate non-tender.  
- **Splitter:** DRE – tender, swollen boggy prostate suggests acute prostatitis; firm, nodular suggests malignancy; normal suggests non-prostatic cause.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** urine dip (nitrite, leucocytes, blood), DRE (if no contraindications), bladder scan for residual volume, urinalysis.  
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U&Es, creatinine, blood cultures (if febrile/septic), PSA (consider but note false elevation).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) if suspicion of abscess or refractory symptoms.  
  - Second-line: CT pelvis if systemic unwell or abscess suspected.  
- **Special tests:** STI screening (Chlamydia, gonorrhoea) if at risk – first-catch urine NAAT. Mid-stream urine culture and sensitivity.  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis based on symptoms, DRE, urine culture. For chronic prostatitis, NIH Chronic Prostatitis Symptom Index (CPSI) can quantify symptoms.  
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures before antibiotics in febrile/septic patient. Also, PSA should be interpreted with caution (elevated in prostatitis; recheck after treatment).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If sepsis: IV fluids, IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2g IV per local sepsis protocol), catheter if retention (prefer suprapubic to avoid urethral trauma), analgesia.  
  - Send cultures before antibiotics if possible – do NOT delay if shocked.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Switch to oral antibiotics when stable (ciprofloxacin or co-trimoxazole).  
  - Consider alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) if retention/voiding difficulty.  
  - NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) for pain.  
  - Monitor temperature, urinary symptoms, repeat urine culture if no response.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Complete antibiotic course: acute 10–14 days (severe/chronic 14–28 days); chronic prostatitis 4–6 weeks.  
  - Alpha-blocker for LUTS, referral to urology if recurrent/no improvement.  
  - Lifestyle advice: avoid caffeine/alcohol, cycling, prolonged sitting; sitz baths.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 ml/hr (adjust for shock/renal).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if >60 or reduced mobility (check bleeding risk).  
  - Diet: oral fluids encouraged unless NBM for procedure.  
  - Observations: baseline then 4–6 hourly (NEWS2 q1h if septic, then reduce).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (acute bacterial):**  
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD for 14 days.  
  - Alternative: ofloxacin 200 mg PO BD for 14 days.  
  - If fluoroquinolone contraindicated: co-trimoxazole 960 mg PO BD for 14 days (check local resistance).  
  - For sepsis (IV): ceftriaxone 2 g IV OD ± gentamicin [check local policy].  
- **Chronic prostatitis:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD or ofloxacin 200 mg PO BD for 4–6 weeks.  
  - Or doxycycline 100 mg PO BD for 4 weeks if atypical organisms suspected.  
- **Alpha-blocker:** tamsulosin 400 micrograms PO OD for LUTS/retention (usually 4–6 weeks).  
- **NSAID:** ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS with food, or naproxen 250–500 mg PO BD. Consider PPI cover if risk.  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin – avoid in epilepsy, tendon disorders, pregnancy, children; risk of tendon rupture (especially elderly + steroids).  
  - Co-trimoxazole – caution in elderly, renal impairment, folate deficiency; avoid with ACE-i/ARBs (hyperkalemia).  
  - Tamsulosin – postural hypotension, intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (cataract surgery).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g., 250 mg BD).  
  - Co-trimoxazole: avoid if CrCl <15, adjust if <30.  
  - NSAIDs: avoid/use caution in renal impairment.  
  - Tamsulosin: caution in hepatic impairment.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - Ciprofloxacin – tendinopathy / QT prolongation; avoid concurrent QT-prolonging drugs.  
  - Co-trimoxazole – hyperkalemia with ACE-i/ARBs.  
  - Tamsulosin – first-dose hypotension (take at bedtime).

### Complications
- **Acute:** prostatic abscess (drainage needed), sepsis/septic shock, acute urinary retention, AKI, epididymo-orchitis, bacteraemia.  
- **Chronic:** chronic pelvic pain syndrome, recurrent UTIs, sexual dysfunction, infertility, psychological distress, prostatic calcifications.

### Prognosis
- Acute bacterial prostatitis: good with appropriate antibiotics; recurrence ~10–20%.  
- Chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome: often persistent; up to 50% have ongoing symptoms at 1 year despite treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always perform DRE in suspected prostatitis (after checking for contraindicating signs like cauda equina).  
- Send urine culture before starting antibiotics – but do NOT delay if septic.  
- Use suprapubic catheter (or smallest urethral catheter) for acute urinary retention to reduce trauma/bacteraemia.  
- Recheck PSA 6 weeks after finishing antibiotics to reset baseline.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do NOT massage the prostate vigorously during DRE in acute prostatitis – can cause bacteraemia.  
- Do NOT delay antibiotics in a septic patient to wait for cultures – give empiric IV broad-spectrum after blood cultures.  
- Do NOT use a large-bore urethral catheter in retention – prefer suprapubic or small temporary catheter.  
- Do NOT assume chronic prostatitis is always bacterial – many cases are non-bacterial chronic pelvic pain syndrome; avoid repeated long antibiotic courses.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever persists >72 hours on oral antibiotics; unable to pass urine; severe perineal pain; signs of sepsis (rigors, confusion, hypotension); new back pain or leg weakness (suspect spinal/epidural abscess).  
- **Follow-up:** Urology outpatient in 4
